concepts.summary: Position describes an object's location relative to a reference point. Distance is the total path length traveled by an object, while displacement is the shortest straight-line distance from initial to final position. Distance is a scalar quantity while displacement is a vector quantity.

Uniform motion occurs when an object covers equal distances in equal intervals of time, meaning its velocity is constant. Non-uniform motion occurs when an object covers unequal distances in equal intervals of time, indicating changing velocity.
Speed is the distance traveled per unit time and is a scalar quantity. Average speed is the total distance traveled divided by the total time taken. Speed does not specify direction of motion.

Velocity is the displacement per unit time and specifies both magnitude and direction of motion. Average velocity is the total displacement divided by total time. For uniformly accelerated motion, average velocity can be calculated as the arithmetic mean of initial and final velocities.
Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity per unit time, calculated as (change in velocity)/(time interval). It can be positive (speeding up) or negative (slowing down). The SI unit is m/s².

Uniform acceleration occurs when velocity changes by equal amounts in equal intervals of time, producing constant acceleration. Non-uniform acceleration occurs when velocity changes at a non-constant rate, producing variable acceleration.

Distance-time graphs display distance on the y-axis and time on the x-axis. A straight line indicates uniform motion, while a curved line indicates non-uniform motion. The slope of the line represents the speed of the object.

Velocity-time graphs display velocity on the y-axis and time on the x-axis. The area under the curve represents the displacement of the object. A horizontal line indicates uniform velocity, while a sloped line indicates acceleration.

The first equation of motion is v = u + at, which relates final velocity (v), initial velocity (u), acceleration (a), and time (t) for uniformly accelerated motion. It represents the velocity-time relationship.

The second equation of motion is s = ut + (1/2)at², which relates displacement (s), initial velocity (u), acceleration (a), and time (t) for uniformly accelerated motion. It represents the position-time relationship.

The third equation of motion is v² = u² + 2as, which relates final velocity (v), initial velocity (u), acceleration (a), and displacement (s) for uniformly accelerated motion. It is derived by eliminating time from the first two equations.

Uniform circular motion occurs when an object moves along a circular path with constant speed. Although the speed remains constant, the velocity changes continuously because direction changes at every point. This motion is accelerated motion because acceleration involves any change in velocity.

In uniform circular motion, speed is calculated as the circumference (2πr) divided by the time period (t) taken to complete one full circle. Speed remains constant in uniform circular motion despite the continuous change in direction.

concepts.concept_name: Graphical Method for Deriving Equations of Motion
concepts.summary: The equations of motion can be derived graphically using velocity-time graphs. The slope of the graph gives acceleration, the area under the graph gives displacement, and the relationships between variables can be visualized geometrically.

concepts.concept_name: Concept of Force and Its Effects
concepts.summary: Force is a push, pull, or hit that can change an object's state of motion or rest, or deform it. Force is observed through its effects rather than directly, and can change both the magnitude and direction of velocity.

Balanced forces are equal and opposite forces that do not change an object's state of motion or rest. Unbalanced forces have different magnitudes and cause a change in the object's motion in the direction of the greater force.

concepts.concept_name: Newton's First Law of Motion (Law of Inertia)
concepts.summary: An object remains in a state of rest or uniform motion in a straight line unless compelled to change by an unbalanced external force. This law is also called the law of inertia, describing the tendency of objects to resist changes in their state of motion.

Inertia is the natural tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion or rest. Mass is the quantitative measure of inertia; objects with greater mass have greater inertia and require larger forces to change their motion.
Momentum is the product of an object's mass and velocity (p = mv). It has both magnitude and direction, with the same direction as velocity. It represents the quantity of motion an object possesses.

The rate of change of momentum of an object is proportional to the applied unbalanced force in the direction of that force. Mathematically expressed as F = ma, it relates force, mass, and acceleration.

Force equals mass times acceleration (F = ma). A larger force produces greater acceleration for the same mass, and a larger mass requires greater force for the same acceleration. This is the quantitative form of Newton's second law.

The SI unit of force is the newton (N), defined as the force that produces an acceleration of 1 m/s² on a mass of 1 kg. One newton equals 1 kg·m/s². Force is measured quantitatively using this standard unit.

concepts.concept_name: Change in Momentum and Time Duration
concepts.summary: The change in momentum depends on both the magnitude of the applied force and the time duration for which it acts. A continuous force over longer time produces greater change in momentum than a sudden force of equal magnitude.

When one object exerts a force on another object, the second object instantaneously exerts an equal and opposite force on the first. These action-reaction forces act on different objects and never cancel each other.

concepts.concept_name: Action and Reaction Forces on Different Objects
concepts.summary: Action and reaction forces are always equal in magnitude but opposite in direction, and they act on two different objects. Although equal, they may produce different accelerations because the objects they act on have different masses.

Friction is a force that acts opposite to the direction of motion and arises between two surfaces in contact. It resists motion and can balance applied forces, preventing motion or causing objects to slow down and come to rest.

concepts.concept_name: Application of Second Law: Catching Moving Objects
concepts.summary: When catching a fast-moving object, increasing the time interval over which the object's velocity decreases reduces the rate of change of momentum and thus the impact force. This principle is applied in catching, jumping, and collision scenarios.

When one object exerts a force on another (like a gun firing a bullet), the second object exerts an equal and opposite force. Despite equal forces, the accelerations differ due to different masses, illustrating why a gun recoils less than a bullet accelerates.

concepts.concept_name: Deriving First Law from Second Law
concepts.summary: When the net external force on an object is zero (F = 0), the second law equation F = ma shows that acceleration is zero. This means the object maintains constant velocity or remains at rest, which is the statement of the first law of motion.

Every object in the universe attracts every other object with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. This force acts along the line joining the centers of the two objects.
G is the universal gravitational constant with a value of 6.673 × 10⁻¹¹ N m² kg⁻². It relates the gravitational force between two objects to their masses and distance. The SI unit is N m² kg⁻².

The centripetal force acts toward the center of a circular path and keeps an object moving in a circle. Without this force, an object would move in a straight line tangent to the circle. The moon orbits Earth due to centripetal force provided by Earth's gravitational pull.

Acceleration due to gravity is the acceleration experienced by an object due to Earth's gravitational force. Its value near Earth's surface is 9.8 m/s², and it is independent of the object's mass. It varies with location and decreases with altitude and from poles to equator.

Free fall occurs when an object moves under the influence of gravitational force alone, with no other forces acting on it. All objects in free fall experience the same acceleration (g), regardless of their mass, and reach the ground simultaneously in the absence of air resistance.
Mass is the measure of the inertia of an object and remains constant everywhere, whether on Earth, the Moon, or in space. It does not change with location and is different from weight.

Weight is the force with which Earth attracts an object toward itself, given by W = mg. It is measured in newtons and acts vertically downward. Unlike mass, weight varies with location because g varies with location.

An object's weight on the Moon is approximately one-sixth of its weight on Earth because the Moon's mass is much smaller than Earth's mass. The weight is calculated using the Moon's gravitational acceleration (about 1.6 m/s²).

Thrust is the force acting perpendicular to a surface, measured in newtons. Pressure is the thrust per unit area, measured in pascals (Pa). The same thrust produces different effects on different areas, which explains why sharp objects penetrate more easily than blunt ones.

Buoyant force is the upward force exerted by a fluid on an object immersed in it. It acts vertically upward and its magnitude equals the weight of the fluid displaced by the object, as stated in Archimedes' principle.

When a body is immersed fully or partially in a fluid, it experiences an upward force (buoyant force) equal to the weight of the fluid displaced. This principle applies to all fluids and is used to determine if objects float or sink.

Density is mass per unit volume. Objects with density less than the fluid float on its surface; objects with density greater than the fluid sink. This occurs because the buoyant force depends on the fluid's density, not the object's material.

The standard kinematic equations (v = u + gt, s = ut + ½gt², v² = u² + 2gs) apply to objects moving under gravitational force when acceleration a is replaced by g. These equations solve problems of free fall, upward motion, and projectile motion vertically.

concepts.concept_name: Variation of g with Altitude and Latitude
concepts.summary: The acceleration due to gravity decreases with altitude (height above Earth's surface) and varies with latitude. The value of g is greater at the poles than at the equator because Earth is not a perfect sphere and the radius varies.

Pressure in a confined fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions. A fluid exerts pressure on all surfaces it contacts, and this pressure increases with depth in liquids due to the weight of the fluid above.

concepts.concept_name: Work done by a constant force in the direction of displacement
concepts.summary: Work is defined as the product of force and displacement when the force acts in the direction of motion: W = F × s. Work is measured in joules (J), where 1 J = 1 N × 1 m. Work is done only when both force and displacement are present.

Work is positive when force acts in the direction of displacement, and negative when force acts opposite to displacement. The sign of work indicates the direction relationship between force and motion of the object.

concepts.concept_name: Concept of energy and its definition
concepts.summary: Energy is the capacity of an object to do work. It is measured in joules (J), the same unit as work. An object with energy can exert force on another object and transfer energy to it, causing work to be done.

Kinetic energy is the energy possessed by a moving object. It is given by KE = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. Kinetic energy depends on both mass and the square of velocity, so doubling velocity quadruples kinetic energy.

The work done by a force on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy: W = ½m(v² - u²). This theorem connects work and kinetic energy, showing that work changes an object's velocity and kinetic energy.

Potential energy is energy stored due to an object's position or configuration. Gravitational potential energy is given by PE = mgh, where m is mass, g is gravitational acceleration, and h is height above a reference level.

Energy stored in elastic objects (like stretched rubber bands, compressed springs, or bent bows) due to change in shape or configuration. This energy can be released to do work, such as propelling an arrow or moving a toy car.

Mechanical energy is the sum of kinetic and potential energy: E = KE + PE. During free fall without air resistance, mechanical energy remains constant as potential energy converts to kinetic energy and vice versa, demonstrating energy conservation.

Energy cannot be created or destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another. The total energy of an isolated system remains constant before and after any transformation, whether from kinetic to potential, chemical to heat, or any other form.

Energy can be converted from one form to another in various processes. Examples include photosynthesis (light to chemical energy), falling objects (potential to kinetic), and electrical heaters (electrical to heat energy).
Power is the rate of doing work or the rate of energy transfer: P = W/t. It is measured in watts (W), where 1 W = 1 J/s. Power indicates how fast an agent does work or consumes energy.

Average power is calculated by dividing total work done or total energy consumed by the total time taken: P_avg = Total energy/Total time. It is useful when power varies with time, providing an overall measure of performance.

concepts.concept_name: Relationship between power and energy consumption
concepts.summary: Energy consumed by a device is the product of its power rating and the time of operation: E = P × t. A 1000 W device running for 1 second consumes 1000 J of energy, allowing calculation of total energy use from power ratings.

concepts.concept_name: Path independence of work done by gravity
concepts.summary: The work done by gravity depends only on the vertical height difference between initial and final positions, not on the path taken. An object raised from point A to B does the same work against gravity regardless of whether the path is straight, curved, or indirect.

Gravitational potential energy is measured relative to a chosen reference or zero level, typically the ground. The same object at a given position can have different potential energy values depending on which reference level is selected.
Sound is produced when objects vibrate. Vibration is rapid to-and-fro motion that creates disturbances in the medium. Examples include vibrating tuning forks, vocal cords, and plucked strings.

Sound travels through matter (solid, liquid, or gas) as a mechanical wave. The vibrating object sets neighboring particles in motion, creating a chain reaction that carries the disturbance forward without particles themselves moving with the wave.

Sound propagates as alternating regions of high pressure (compressions) where particles are crowded, and low pressure (rarefactions) where particles are spread apart. These occur as vibrating objects push and pull the medium.

Sound waves are longitudinal waves where particles oscillate parallel to the direction of wave propagation. Particles move back and forth in the same direction as the disturbance travels, not perpendicular to it.
Wavelength (λ) is the distance between two consecutive compressions or two consecutive rarefactions in a sound wave. It is measured in meters and represents one complete cycle of the wave.
Frequency (ν) is the number of complete oscillations of density/pressure per unit time, measured in Hertz. It counts how many compressions or rarefactions pass a fixed point per second.

Time period (T) is the time taken for one complete oscillation of the medium, or the time between successive compressions/rarefactions passing a fixed point. It is inversely related to frequency (T = 1/ν).
Amplitude is the maximum disturbance of particles from their mean position during oscillation. It directly determines the loudness of sound—larger amplitude means louder sound.

concepts.concept_name: Speed of Sound in Different Media
concepts.summary: Sound travels at different speeds depending on the medium and its temperature. Speed increases in denser media and increases with temperature. Solids have higher sound speed than liquids, which have higher speed than gases.

concepts.concept_name: Relationship Between Speed, Wavelength, and Frequency
concepts.summary: The fundamental wave equation v = λν relates speed (v), wavelength (λ), and frequency (ν). Speed of sound remains constant in a given medium under same conditions, while wavelength and frequency are inversely related.

Pitch is how the brain perceives the frequency of sound. Higher frequency produces higher pitch, while lower frequency produces lower pitch. Objects with different sizes and conditions vibrate at different frequencies to produce different pitches.

Loudness is the physiological perception of sound intensity. It is primarily determined by the amplitude of the sound wave—larger amplitude means louder sound. Loudness decreases as sound travels farther from the source.

Intensity is the amount of sound energy passing through unit area per unit time, measured in watts per square meter. It is a physical property different from loudness, which is a physiological response of the ear.

Timbre is the characteristic that allows distinction between sounds of the same pitch and loudness from different sources. A tone is a single frequency sound, while a note is a mixture of frequencies, producing pleasant timbre.

Sound reflects off solid or liquid surfaces following the same law of reflection as light: angle of incidence equals angle of reflection. An obstacle of large size is needed for effective sound reflection.

An echo is the reflected sound heard as a distinct repetition of the original sound. For a distinct echo, the time interval between original and reflected sound must be at least 0.1 s. Minimum obstacle distance = (speed × 0.1 s)/2.
Reverberation is the persistence of sound in a large hall due to repeated reflections from walls until sound becomes inaudible. It can be reduced by covering surfaces with sound-absorbent materials.
Human hearing extends from 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz). Frequencies below 20 Hz are infrasonic, frequencies above 20 kHz are ultrasonic. Hearing sensitivity decreases for higher frequencies with age.

Ultrasound refers to sound waves with frequencies above 20 kHz. It has applications in medical imaging (ultrasound scanning, echocardiography), cleaning, detecting flaws in metals, and breaking kidney stones due to its high frequency and well-defined paths.

Infrasound refers to sound waves with frequencies below 20 Hz, inaudible to humans. Many animals like whales, elephants, and rhinoceroses produce infrasound for communication. Earthquakes produce infrasound that may alert animals.

Light reflects off surfaces following two laws: the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection, and the incident ray, normal, and reflected ray all lie in the same plane. These laws apply to all reflecting surfaces including curved mirrors.
A plane mirror always forms a virtual, erect image that is the same size as the object and located as far behind the mirror as the object is in front. The image is laterally inverted.

concepts.concept_name: Spherical Mirrors - Concave and Convex Types
concepts.summary: Spherical mirrors have curved reflecting surfaces forming part of a sphere. Concave mirrors curve inward (center of curvature in front) and converge light, while convex mirrors curve outward (center of curvature behind) and diverge light.

concepts.concept_name: Focal Length and Principal Focus of Spherical Mirrors
concepts.summary: The principal focus is the point where parallel rays converge (concave) or appear to diverge from (convex). Focal length is the distance from the pole to the principal focus, and equals half the radius of curvature (f = R/2).

concepts.concept_name: Spherical Mirror Image Formation - Concave Mirror
concepts.summary: Concave mirrors form different types of images depending on object position: real and inverted images when object is beyond focus, virtual and erect when between pole and focus. Image size varies from highly diminished to magnified.

concepts.concept_name: Spherical Mirror Image Formation - Convex Mirror
concepts.summary: Convex mirrors always form virtual, erect, and diminished images regardless of object position. Images appear between the pole and focus, behind the mirror.

concepts.concept_name: Ray Diagram Method for Image Location in Mirrors
concepts.summary: Ray diagrams use specific rays (parallel to axis, through focus, through center of curvature, through pole) to locate images formed by spherical mirrors. The intersection of at least two reflected rays gives the image position.

New Cartesian Sign Convention: pole is origin, object is left of mirror, distances right are positive and left are negative, distances above principal axis are positive and below are negative. Essential for using mirror formula correctly.

Mirror formula 1/f = 1/v + 1/u relates focal length, image distance, and object distance. Magnification m = h'/h = -v/u gives the ratio of image height to object height, with sign indicating image nature (real or virtual).

Refraction is the bending of light when it travels obliquely from one transparent medium to another. Light bends toward the normal when entering a denser medium and away from normal when entering a rarer medium.

When light passes through a rectangular glass slab, it refracts at both air-glass and glass-air interfaces. The emergent ray is parallel to the incident ray but laterally displaced due to equal and opposite bending at parallel surfaces.

concepts.concept_name: Laws of Refraction and Snell's Law
concepts.summary: Laws of refraction: incident ray, refracted ray, and normal lie in same plane. Snell's law states sin(i)/sin(r) = constant = refractive index, where i is angle of incidence and r is angle of refraction.

Refractive index n is the ratio of speed of light in one medium to another, or for absolute refractive index, speed in vacuum/air to speed in medium. Optical density determines how much a medium bends light; higher refractive index means optically denser.

concepts.concept_name: Spherical Lenses - Convex and Concave Types
concepts.summary: Lenses are transparent materials bounded by two spherical surfaces. Convex (double convex) lenses are thicker at center and converge light. Concave (double concave) lenses are thicker at edges and diverge light.

concepts.concept_name: Focal Length and Principal Focus of Lenses
concepts.summary: Lenses have two principal foci F1 and F2 equidistant from optical center. Focal length f is distance from optical center to principal focus. Convex lenses have positive focal length; concave lenses have negative focal length.

Convex lenses form real, inverted images when object is beyond focus (image varies from diminished to enlarged), and virtual, erect, enlarged images when object is between focus and lens. Image characteristics depend on object position relative to 2F and F.
Concave lenses always form virtual, erect, and diminished images irrespective of object position. Image appears on the same side as object, between focal point and optical center.

Lens formula 1/f = 1/v - 1/u relates focal length, image distance, and object distance. Magnification m = h'/h = v/u, where positive m indicates virtual image and negative m indicates real inverted image.

Power P = 1/f (in meters) is measured in diopters (D). Convex lenses have positive power; concave lenses have negative power. Power is more convenient than focal length for opticians prescribing corrective lenses and combining multiple lenses.

concepts.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
concepts.concept_name: Power of Accommodation of the Eye
concepts.summary: The ability of the eye lens to change its focal length by adjusting its curvature through ciliary muscles to focus on objects at different distances. This allows clear vision of both near and distant objects.

concepts.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
concepts.concept_name: Near Point and Far Point of the Eye
concepts.summary: The near point is the minimum distance (about 25 cm) at which the eye can see objects clearly without strain. The far point is the maximum distance (infinity for normal eye) up to which the eye can see clearly.

concepts.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
concepts.summary: A refractive defect where distant objects appear blurred while nearby objects are seen clearly, caused by excessive lens curvature or eyeball elongation. It is corrected using a concave lens to converge light rays onto the retina.

concepts.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
concepts.summary: A refractive defect where nearby objects appear blurred while distant objects are seen clearly, caused by insufficient lens curvature or short eyeball. It is corrected using a convex lens to provide additional focusing power.

concepts.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
concepts.summary: A refractive defect occurring with age due to weakening of ciliary muscles and reduced lens flexibility, making it difficult to see nearby objects clearly. It is corrected using bifocal lenses with concave portion for distant vision and convex portion for near vision.

concepts.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
concepts.concept_name: Structure and Function of the Human Eye
concepts.summary: The human eye functions like a camera with the cornea, lens, and retina working together to form inverted real images. The iris controls pupil size to regulate light entry, and the retina contains light-sensitive cells that send signals to the brain via optic nerves.

concepts.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
concepts.concept_name: Refraction of Light through a Prism
concepts.summary: When light passes through a triangular prism with inclined refracting surfaces, it bends at both surfaces following Snell's law. The emergent ray deviates from the incident ray by an angle called the angle of deviation.

concepts.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
concepts.concept_name: Dispersion of White Light and Spectrum Formation
concepts.summary: White light splits into its seven component colours (violet, indigo, blue, green, yellow, orange, red) when passed through a prism because different colours refract through different angles. The band of separated colours is called the spectrum.

concepts.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
concepts.summary: A rainbow is a natural spectrum formed when sunlight is refracted, dispersed, and internally reflected by tiny water droplets in the atmosphere. It always appears in the direction opposite to the Sun due to these optical phenomena.

concepts.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
concepts.summary: When light strikes fine particles in a colloidal solution or atmosphere (dust, smoke, water droplets), the light path becomes visible as it is scattered diffusely by these particles. This phenomenon is called the Tyndall effect.

concepts.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
concepts.concept_name: Blue Colour of the Sky and Light Scattering
concepts.summary: The sky appears blue because air molecules and fine particles smaller than visible light wavelengths scatter blue light (shorter wavelengths) more effectively than red light. Without atmospheric scattering, the sky would appear dark.

concepts.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
concepts.summary: As starlight passes through Earth's atmosphere with varying density and refractive index, it undergoes continuous refraction. This causes the apparent position of stars to fluctuate constantly, creating the twinkling effect.

concepts.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
concepts.summary: Planets appear as extended sources of light rather than point sources, so light from different parts averages out to produce a steady intensity at the observer's eye, eliminating the twinkling effect seen for stars.

concepts.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
concepts.summary: Due to atmospheric refraction bending sunlight towards the normal, the Sun becomes visible about 2 minutes before actual sunrise and remains visible for about 2 minutes after actual sunset. The Sun's disc also appears flattened at these times.

concepts.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
concepts.concept_name: Relationship between Particle Size and Scattered Light Wavelength
concepts.summary: Fine particles scatter light of shorter wavelengths (blue) more effectively, while larger particles scatter light of longer wavelengths (red). This size-dependent scattering explains various optical phenomena in nature.

concepts.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
concepts.concept_name: Recombination of Spectrum to Form White Light
concepts.summary: When all seven colours of the spectrum are passed through an inverted prism or converging lens, they recombine to form white light again, demonstrating that white light is composed of these component colours.

Electric current is the flow of electric charge through a conductor, defined as the amount of charge flowing per unit time (I = Q/t). It is measured in amperes (A), where 1 A = 1 C/s. Conventionally, current direction is opposite to electron flow.

An electric circuit is a continuous and closed path through which electric current flows. A complete circuit requires a source of potential difference, conducting wires, and a load. Breaking the circuit at any point stops current flow.

Potential difference is the work done per unit charge to move a charge from one point to another in an electric circuit. It is measured in volts (V), where 1 V = 1 J/C. It drives the flow of current in a circuit.

Ohm's law states that the potential difference across a conductor is directly proportional to the current flowing through it at constant temperature: V = IR. This relationship holds for ohmic conductors and helps predict circuit behavior.

Resistance is a material property that opposes the flow of electric charge through a conductor. Measured in ohms (Ω), it is defined as R = V/I. Higher resistance leads to lower current for a given potential difference.

concepts.concept_name: Factors Affecting Resistance of a Conductor
concepts.summary: The resistance of a uniform conductor depends on three factors: (i) directly proportional to length (l), (ii) inversely proportional to cross-sectional area (A), and (iii) on the material's resistivity (ρ). Formula: R = ρl/A.

Resistivity (ρ) is a characteristic property of a material that determines its ability to resist electric current. It is measured in Ω·m. Different materials have different resistivities: metals have low resistivity (10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁶ Ω·m), while insulators have very high resistivity (10¹² to 10¹⁷ Ω·m).

When resistors are connected end-to-end in series, the same current flows through each. The total resistance is the sum of individual resistances: Rs = R₁ + R₂ + R₃. The total potential difference equals the sum of individual potential differences.

When resistors are connected in parallel, the potential difference across each is the same. The reciprocal of equivalent resistance equals the sum of reciprocals of individual resistances: 1/Rp = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃. Total current is the sum of individual currents.
The heat produced in a resistor is given by H = I²Rt = VIt = V²t/R. Heat is directly proportional to the square of current, the resistance, and the time for which current flows through the resistor.

When current flows through a resistor, electrical energy is converted into heat due to resistance. This effect is used in practical applications like electric heaters, toasters, and bulb filaments. It can also be undesirable, causing temperature rise in circuit components.

Electric power is the rate of energy consumption in an electric circuit, given by P = VI = I²R = V²/R. It is measured in watts (W), where 1 W = 1 V × 1 A. Power indicates how quickly electrical energy is being used or dissipated.

concepts.concept_name: Electrical Energy and its Commercial Unit
concepts.summary: Electrical energy is the product of power and time: E = Pt. The commercial unit is kilowatt-hour (kWh), where 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J. This unit is used to measure household electricity consumption and billing.
An ammeter is an instrument used to measure electric current in a circuit. It must be connected in series with the circuit element through which current is to be measured to ensure accurate readings.

A voltmeter is an instrument used to measure potential difference between two points in a circuit. It must be connected in parallel across the two points between which the potential difference is to be measured.

A fuse is a safety device made of wire with a low melting point that protects circuits from excessive current. When current exceeds a safe limit, the fuse wire melts and breaks the circuit. Fuses are rated by current capacity (1A, 2A, 5A, etc.).

concepts.concept_name: Advantages of Parallel Circuits over Series Circuits
concepts.summary: In parallel circuits, each device receives the full potential difference and can draw the required current independently. If one device fails, others continue to work. Series circuits have the disadvantage that all devices share the same current and failure of one breaks the entire circuit.

A rheostat is a variable resistance device used to regulate current in an electric circuit without changing the voltage source. By adjusting its resistance, the current flowing through the circuit can be controlled to desired levels.

concepts.concept_name: Choice of Materials for Electrical Devices
concepts.summary: Different materials are chosen for specific electrical applications based on their properties. Alloys like nichrome are used in heating devices for high melting point and resistance to oxidation. Tungsten is used for bulb filaments. Copper and aluminum are used for transmission lines due to low resistivity.

A magnetic field is the region surrounding a magnet where magnetic force can be detected. It has both magnitude and direction, with direction conventionally taken from north pole to south pole outside the magnet. Magnetic field strength is indicated by the closeness of field lines.

concepts.concept_name: Magnetic Field Lines and Their Characteristics
concepts.summary: Magnetic field lines are curves representing the path a free north pole would take in a magnetic field. Field lines emerge from north poles and merge into south poles, form closed curves, and never intersect. Closer spacing indicates stronger field regions.

An electric current flowing through a conductor produces a magnetic field around it. The magnitude of this magnetic field depends on the current strength and inversely on the distance from the conductor. This demonstrates the link between electricity and magnetism.

concepts.concept_name: Right-Hand Thumb Rule for Magnetic Field Direction
concepts.summary: When a current-carrying conductor is held with the thumb pointing in the direction of current, the fingers wrap around showing the direction of magnetic field lines. This rule determines the direction of magnetic field produced by a straight current-carrying conductor.

concepts.concept_name: Magnetic Field Around a Straight Current-Carrying Conductor
concepts.summary: A straight wire carrying current produces concentric circular magnetic field lines centered on the wire. The field strength increases with current and decreases with distance from the wire. The direction follows the right-hand thumb rule.

concepts.concept_name: Magnetic Field in a Circular Current Loop
concepts.summary: A circular loop of current-carrying wire produces magnetic field lines that are straight at the center of the loop and curved outside. The field direction at the center is perpendicular to the plane of the loop, determinable by the right-hand rule.

A solenoid is a coil of many insulated wire turns wrapped in a cylinder shape. It produces a uniform magnetic field inside with parallel field lines, similar to a bar magnet with distinct north and south poles at the ends.

An electromagnet consists of a soft iron core wrapped with insulated copper wire coil. When current flows, it produces a strong magnetic field that magnetizes the iron. The strength depends on current and number of coil turns.

concepts.concept_name: Force on a Current-Carrying Conductor in a Magnetic Field
concepts.summary: A current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a mechanical force perpendicular to both the current direction and field direction. The force increases with current strength, field strength, and conductor length.

When the thumb, forefinger, and middle finger of the left hand are held mutually perpendicular, with forefinger pointing in field direction and middle finger in current direction, the thumb indicates the force direction on the conductor.

concepts.concept_name: Dependence of Magnetic Field on Current and Distance
concepts.summary: The magnetic field produced by a current-carrying conductor is directly proportional to the current magnitude and inversely proportional to the distance from the conductor. Increasing current or decreasing distance strengthens the field.

A domestic circuit consists of a live wire (red insulation, positive), neutral wire (black insulation, negative), and earth wire (green insulation). The potential difference between live and neutral is 220V. Circuits are protected by fuses and switches.

A fuse is a safety device containing a wire that melts when excessive current flows, breaking the circuit. It protects appliances from damage due to short-circuiting or overloading caused by excessive current or voltage spikes.

concepts.concept_name: Earth Wire and Safety in Domestic Circuits
concepts.summary: The earth wire (green insulation) connects metallic bodies of appliances to a metal plate deep in the ground. It provides a low-resistance path for leakage current, preventing electric shock by maintaining the appliance body at earth potential.

A short circuit occurs when live and neutral wires come into direct contact due to damaged insulation or appliance fault, causing abrupt current increase. Overloading also occurs when too many high-power appliances are connected simultaneously.

concepts.concept_name: Parallel Connection of Appliances in Domestic Circuits
concepts.summary: Household appliances are connected in parallel between live and neutral wires, ensuring each appliance receives the full 220V potential difference. This allows independent operation of appliances through individual switches.

concepts.concept_name: Effect of Multiple Turns on Magnetic Field in Coils
concepts.summary: A coil with n turns produces a magnetic field n times stronger than a single turn because the field from each turn adds up in the same direction. This principle is used to increase electromagnet strength.

Physical quantities are measured using fundamental (base) units for primary quantities like length, mass, and time, while derived units are combinations of base units used for other quantities. Understanding which units are fundamental versus derived is essential for measurement and dimensional analysis.

The International System of Units (SI) is the globally accepted standard system with seven base units: metre, kilogram, second, ampere, kelvin, mole, and candela. SI units use decimal conversions making calculations simpler and more consistent across all scientific work.

Significant figures represent the reliable digits plus the first uncertain digit in a measurement, indicating the precision of measurement. The number of significant figures depends on the least count of the measuring instrument and should be properly maintained in reported results.

Specific rules determine which digits are significant: all non-zero digits are significant, zeros between non-zero digits are significant, leading zeros are not significant, and trailing zeros are significant only when a decimal point is present. Scientific notation eliminates ambiguity in counting significant figures.

In multiplication or division, the result should have as many significant figures as the measurement with the least significant figures. In addition or subtraction, the result should have as many decimal places as the measurement with the least decimal places.

When a digit less than 5 is dropped, the preceding digit remains unchanged. When a digit greater than 5 is dropped, the preceding digit increases by 1. When exactly 5 is dropped, the preceding digit is rounded to the nearest even number. This ensures consistent treatment of measurement uncertainty.

Absolute error is the uncertainty in a measured quantity (±value), while relative error is the ratio of absolute error to the measured value expressed as a percentage. Understanding both types helps assess the quality and reliability of measurements.

When combining measured values through arithmetic operations, uncertainties from individual measurements propagate through the calculation. For multiplication/division, relative errors add; for addition/subtraction, absolute errors combine differently based on decimal places.

Dimensions describe how a physical quantity depends on the seven base quantities (length [L], mass [M], time [T], electric current [A], temperature [K], luminous intensity [cd], and amount of substance [mol]). Dimensions are expressed as exponents of these base quantities.

A dimensional formula expresses how a physical quantity is composed of base quantities with their respective powers, like [M L² T⁻²] for energy. A dimensional equation equates a physical quantity to its dimensional formula.

In any valid equation, all terms must have the same dimensions. This principle allows checking the dimensional consistency of equations: if dimensions on both sides match after simplification, the equation is dimensionally correct. However, dimensional correctness does not guarantee the equation is physically accurate.

Dimensional analysis is a method to test whether an equation is dimensionally consistent by verifying that both sides have identical dimensions. This preliminary test can rule out incorrect equations, though passing the test does not confirm correctness.

If the dependence of one physical quantity on others is known as a product form, dimensional analysis can be used to find the exponents of each quantity. The method cannot determine dimensionless constants like 2π or numerical factors in the relationship.

Dimensional analysis cannot determine dimensionless constants or numerical multipliers in equations. It also cannot distinguish between quantities with identical dimensions. Arguments of special functions like trigonometric and logarithmic functions must be dimensionless.

concepts.concept_name: Scientific Notation and Order of Magnitude
concepts.summary: Scientific notation expresses numbers as a × 10ᵇ where a is between 1 and 10. The order of magnitude is the power b, indicating the scale of a quantity. This notation is ideal for reporting measurements as it clarifies significant figures unambiguously.

SI prefixes (like kilo-, milli-, micro-, nano-) represent powers of 10 for unit conversion, making it easy to express very large or very small quantities. Proper use of prefixes and understanding unit relationships enables straightforward conversions within the SI system.

Accuracy refers to how close a measurement is to the true value, while precision refers to the repeatability of measurements. Measurement uncertainty is determined by the least count of the instrument and affects the number of significant figures that should be reported.

concepts.concept_name: Exact Numbers and Infinite Significant Figures
concepts.summary: Exact numbers that appear in formulas (like 2 in 2πr or the number of sides in geometric shapes) have infinite significant figures and do not limit the precision of calculated results. Mathematical constants like π and e are known to many significant figures.

concepts.concept_name: Position and Displacement in One Dimension
concepts.summary: Position specifies the location of an object with respect to a chosen origin on a straight line. Displacement is the change in position and is a vector quantity that can be positive or negative depending on direction chosen.

Average velocity is total displacement divided by time interval and is a vector quantity. Average speed is total path length divided by time interval and is always positive or zero. Average speed is greater than or equal to the magnitude of average velocity.

Instantaneous velocity is the limiting value of average velocity as the time interval approaches zero. It represents how fast an object is moving at a specific instant and equals the slope of the tangent to the position-time graph at that point.

Instantaneous speed is the magnitude of instantaneous velocity at any given instant. Unlike average speed, instantaneous speed is always equal to the magnitude of instantaneous velocity at that instant.
Average acceleration is the change in velocity divided by the time interval over which the change occurs. On a velocity-time graph, it represents the slope of the line connecting two velocity points.

Instantaneous acceleration is the limiting value of average acceleration as the time interval approaches zero. It represents the rate of change of velocity at a specific instant and equals the slope of the tangent to the velocity-time graph.

Uniform acceleration occurs when velocity changes at a constant rate with time, making the velocity-time graph a straight line. Non-uniform acceleration occurs when the rate of velocity change varies with time.

Position-time graphs show how an object's location changes with time. A straight line indicates constant velocity, a parabola indicates constant acceleration, and the slope at any point gives instantaneous velocity.
Velocity-time graphs show how velocity changes with time. The slope of the graph gives instantaneous acceleration, and the area under the curve represents the displacement during that time interval.

concepts.concept_name: Kinematic Equations for Uniformly Accelerated Motion
concepts.summary: Kinematic equations relate five quantities: displacement (x), time (t), initial velocity (v₀), final velocity (v), and acceleration (a) for motion with constant acceleration. These are: v = v₀ + at, x = v₀t + ½at², and v² = v₀² + 2ax.

Free fall is motion under gravity alone with constant downward acceleration g ≈ 9.8 m/s². It is a special case of uniformly accelerated motion where acceleration is always directed downward regardless of the direction of motion.

Vertical projectile motion involves objects thrown upward or downward with an initial velocity under constant gravitational acceleration. The motion can be analyzed in two phases: upward motion (with decreasing speed) and downward motion (with increasing speed).

Relative velocity is the velocity of one object as observed from the reference frame of another object. For one-dimensional motion, it is calculated by vector subtraction of velocities with respect to the ground.

Stopping distance is the distance traveled by a vehicle before coming to rest after brakes are applied. It depends on initial velocity and deceleration, with stopping distance proportional to the square of initial velocity.

Displacement, velocity, and acceleration are algebraic quantities with signs determined by the chosen positive direction and origin. The sign of acceleration does not directly indicate whether speed is increasing or decreasing; this depends on the relative directions of velocity and acceleration.

When an object changes direction during motion with constant acceleration, velocity changes sign while acceleration remains constant. The object momentarily comes to rest at the point where direction changes.
Acceleration-time graphs show how acceleration varies with time. For uniformly accelerated motion, the graph is a horizontal line, while the area under the curve represents the change in velocity.

Physical quantities are classified as scalars (magnitude only) or vectors (magnitude and direction). Scalars follow ordinary algebra rules, while vectors obey special laws like triangle and parallelogram addition. Understanding this distinction is fundamental to analyzing motion in multiple dimensions.

Vectors are added using the head-to-tail (triangle) method or parallelogram method. Vector addition is commutative (A+B = B+A) and associative. Subtraction is defined as A-B = A+(-B). The resultant depends only on magnitudes and relative directions, not on the order of addition.

Multiplying a vector A by a positive scalar λ changes its magnitude by factor λ while keeping direction same. Multiplying by a negative scalar reverses the direction. The result is always a vector whose dimension is the product of dimensions of the scalar and original vector.

A vector can be resolved into two or more component vectors along chosen directions. Any vector A can be expressed as A = λa + μb along non-collinear vectors a and b. In rectangular coordinates, components along perpendicular axes simplify calculations significantly.

Unit vectors (î, ĵ, k̂) have magnitude 1 and point along x-, y-, z-axes. Any vector A can be written as A = Axî + Ayĵ where Ax and Ay are scalar components. If A makes angle θ with x-axis, then Ax = A cos θ and Ay = A sin θ.

Vectors are added analytically by summing their corresponding components: if R = A + B, then Rx = Ax + Bx and Ry = Ay + By. This method is more accurate than graphical methods and extends easily to multiple vectors and three dimensions.

The position vector r = xî + yĵ specifies location of a particle relative to origin. Displacement Δr = r' - r is the vector from initial to final position, independent of the path taken. Magnitude of displacement ≤ path length.

Average velocity is displacement divided by time interval. Instantaneous velocity v = dr/dt is tangent to the path. In component form, v = vxî + vyĵ where vx = dx/dt and vy = dy/dt. Velocity direction and magnitude change independently in 2D motion.

Average acceleration is change in velocity divided by time interval. Instantaneous acceleration a = dv/dt with components ax = dvx/dt and ay = dvy/dt. In 2D, velocity and acceleration need not be collinear (unlike 1D motion). The direction of a can be at any angle to v.

concepts.concept_name: Motion with Constant Acceleration in a Plane
concepts.summary: When acceleration is constant, velocity changes linearly with time: v = v₀ + at. Position is r = r₀ + v₀t + ½at². Motion can be decomposed into two independent 1D motions along perpendicular axes, each with constant acceleration.

A projectile has constant horizontal velocity (ax = 0) and constant downward acceleration ay = -g. Horizontal and vertical motions are independent. Position and velocity at any time t are: x = v₀cosθ₀·t, y = v₀sinθ₀·t - ½gt², vx = v₀cosθ₀, vy = v₀sinθ₀ - gt.

concepts.concept_name: Equation of Trajectory of a Projectile
concepts.summary: The path of a projectile is parabolic, given by y = (tanθ₀)x - (g/2v₀²cos²θ₀)x². This equation is obtained by eliminating time from the kinematic equations. The parabolic shape is independent of gravitational strength but depends on initial velocity and launch angle.

concepts.concept_name: Range and Maximum Height of Projectile
concepts.summary: Horizontal range R = (v₀²sin2θ₀)/g is maximum at θ₀ = 45°. Maximum height hm = (v₀²sin²θ₀)/(2g). Time of flight Tf = (2v₀sinθ₀)/g. These quantities depend only on initial speed, launch angle, and gravity, not on the path shape.

When an object moves in a circle at constant speed v, it undergoes acceleration directed toward the center. This centripetal acceleration has magnitude ac = v²/R and is always perpendicular to velocity. Speed is constant but direction continuously changes.

Centripetal acceleration ac = v²/R can also be expressed as ac = ω²R, where ω is angular velocity. Angular velocity ω = Δθ/Δt relates to linear velocity by v = ωR. For circular motion: ω = 2πν where ν is frequency and ν = 1/T.

concepts.concept_name: Time Period and Frequency in Circular Motion
concepts.summary: Time period T is the time for one complete revolution. Frequency ν = 1/T is the number of revolutions per second. Linear speed v = 2πR/T = 2πRν and centripetal acceleration ac = 4π²ν²R = 4π²R/T². These relate macroscopic and microscopic descriptions of circular motion.

concepts.concept_name: Newton's First Law of Motion and Inertia
concepts.summary: A body continues in its state of rest or uniform motion unless compelled by an external force. Inertia is the property of a body to resist changes in its state of motion. This law is consistent with Galileo's law of inertia derived from inclined plane experiments.

Momentum is the product of mass and velocity (p = mv) and is a vector quantity. It represents the quantity of motion and is crucial for understanding the effect of forces on bodies of different masses and speeds.

The net external force on a body is directly proportional to the rate of change of its momentum and acts in the direction of acceleration. Expressed as F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration. The SI unit of force is newton (N).

Impulse is the product of force and time duration, equal to the change in momentum (J = FΔt = Δp). Large forces acting for very short durations produce measurable momentum changes, useful in situations like collisions and catches.

Forces always occur in pairs: the force on body A by body B equals and is opposite to the force on body B by body A. These action-reaction forces act simultaneously on different bodies and cannot cancel each other.
In an isolated system with no external forces, the total momentum remains constant. This law follows from Newton's second and third laws and applies to all collisions, whether elastic or inelastic.

concepts.concept_name: Equilibrium of a Particle under Forces
concepts.summary: A particle is in equilibrium when the net external force on it is zero. For equilibrium under multiple forces, the vector sum of all forces must be zero, which can be represented by a closed polygon of forces.

Static friction opposes impending relative motion between surfaces and adjusts itself up to a maximum limit (fs ≤ μsN), where μs is the coefficient of static friction. It prevents motion when applied force is below the limiting friction value.

Kinetic friction opposes actual relative motion between surfaces in contact and is approximately independent of velocity and area of contact. It satisfies fk = μkN, where μk is the coefficient of kinetic friction, which is less than μs.

When bodies are in contact, they exert mutual contact forces on each other. The normal reaction is the component of contact force perpendicular to the surfaces in contact, while friction is the component parallel to the surfaces.
Tension is the force exerted by a string or rope on an object. For an inextensible string of negligible mass, tension is uniform throughout and acts along the length of the string.

When a spring is compressed or extended, it exerts a restoring force proportional to the displacement: F = -kx, where k is the force constant and x is displacement. The negative sign indicates the force opposes the displacement.

For uniform circular motion, centripetal force directed toward the center provides the necessary acceleration: F = mv²/R. This force can be provided by tension, friction, gravity, or other forces depending on the situation.

On a level (unbanked) road, static friction provides the centripetal force needed for circular motion. Maximum speed is limited by μsRg, and friction opposes the outward skidding tendency of the vehicle.

On a banked road, both the normal reaction and friction contribute to provide centripetal force. At optimum speed (where friction is not needed), v₀ = √(Rg tan θ), allowing safer circular motion at higher speeds than on level roads.
Every object on Earth experiences gravitational force (weight = mg) directed downward. This force is always present and must be considered when applying Newton's laws to terrestrial motion.

Free-body diagrams isolate a system and show all external forces acting on it. This systematic approach helps correctly apply Newton's laws by clearly identifying which forces act on the chosen system without including internal forces.

Rolling friction arises when surfaces deform slightly during rolling contact, creating a small area of contact rather than a point. Rolling friction is much smaller (orders of magnitude) than static or kinetic friction for the same normal force.

The scalar product of two vectors A and B is defined as A·B = AB cos θ, where θ is the angle between them. It produces a scalar quantity and can be interpreted as the product of one vector's magnitude and the component of the other vector along it.

concepts.concept_name: Definition of Work by a Constant Force
concepts.summary: Work done by a constant force F on an object undergoing displacement d is defined as W = F·d = Fd cos θ, where θ is the angle between force and displacement. Work is a scalar quantity that can be positive, negative, or zero.

concepts.concept_name: Kinetic Energy and its Relation to Motion
concepts.summary: Kinetic energy of an object with mass m and velocity v is defined as K = ½mv². It is a scalar quantity that measures the capacity of the object to do work by virtue of its motion.
The change in kinetic energy of a particle equals the work done on it by the net force. Mathematically, Kf - Ki = W, where Kf and Ki are final and initial kinetic energies respectively.
For a variable force, work done is calculated as the integral of force over displacement: W = ∫F(x)dx. Graphically, it equals the area under the force-displacement curve.

A conservative force is one where work done is path-independent and depends only on initial and final positions; work done in a closed path is zero. Non-conservative forces like friction have path-dependent work.

Gravitational potential energy of an object at height h above a reference point is V(h) = mgh, where m is mass and g is gravitational acceleration. It represents stored energy due to the object's position.

concepts.concept_name: Elastic Potential Energy of a Spring
concepts.summary: A spring obeying Hooke's law (Fs = -kx) stores elastic potential energy V(x) = ½kx², where k is the spring constant and x is the displacement from equilibrium position.
The total mechanical energy (kinetic plus potential energy) of a system remains constant when only conservative forces act on it. E = K + V = constant along the motion path.
When non-conservative forces like friction act, the change in total mechanical energy equals the work done by non-conservative forces: ΔE = Wnc. This modifies the conservation principle.
Power is the rate at which work is done. Average power P_avg = W/t and instantaneous power P = dW/dt = F·v, where v is instantaneous velocity. Unit is watt (W) or joule per second.
In an elastic collision, both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved. For two objects with masses m1, m2 and initial velocities, the final velocities can be determined using conservation laws.
In an inelastic collision, momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not. In a completely inelastic collision, the objects stick together after collision and move with a common final velocity.

concepts.concept_name: Collisions in Two Dimensions and Momentum Conservation
concepts.summary: In two-dimensional collisions, linear momentum is conserved in both x and y directions. With one initial constraint given (like scattering angle), all final velocities and directions can be determined if the collision is elastic.
Work done is zero when: (i) displacement is zero, (ii) applied force is zero, or (iii) force and displacement are perpendicular (θ = 90°). In these cases, W = Fd cos θ = 0.

Work is positive when force and displacement are in the same general direction (0° < θ < 90°), and negative when they oppose each other (90° < θ ≤ 180°). The sign indicates whether energy is added to or removed from the system.

concepts.concept_name: Relation Between Force and Potential Energy
concepts.summary: For a conservative force in one dimension, the force is related to potential energy by F = -dV/dx. This relationship shows that force acts in the direction of decreasing potential energy.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.concept_name: Centre of Mass of a System of Particles
concepts.summary: The centre of mass is defined as the point whose position is the mass-weighted average of all particle positions in a system. It is calculated using the formula R = (Σ mi ri) / M, where M is the total mass. For extended bodies, this concept allows us to treat the entire system as if concentrated at this single point.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.summary: The centre of mass of a system moves according to Newton's second law applied to the entire system: MA_cm = F_ext. The acceleration of the centre of mass depends only on external forces, not internal forces. Internal forces cancel out due to Newton's third law.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.concept_name: Linear Momentum of a System of Particles
concepts.summary: The total linear momentum of a system equals the product of total mass and velocity of the centre of mass: P = MV_cm. When the total external force on a system is zero, the total linear momentum is conserved, meaning the centre of mass moves with constant velocity.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.concept_name: Vector Product (Cross Product) of Two Vectors
concepts.summary: The vector product a × b produces a vector with magnitude |a||b|sin(θ) perpendicular to both vectors, with direction given by the right-hand rule. Unlike scalar product, vector product is non-commutative: a × b = −(b × a). It is used to define torque and angular momentum.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.concept_name: Angular Velocity and its Relation with Linear Velocity
concepts.summary: Angular velocity ω is the time rate of change of angular displacement (dθ/dt) and is a vector directed along the axis of rotation. The linear velocity of a particle in a rotating rigid body is related to angular velocity by v = ω × r, where r is the position vector from the axis.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.summary: Torque is defined as τ = r × F, where r is the position vector and F is the force. It measures the rotational effect of a force about a point or axis. The magnitude is τ = rF sin(θ), and torque vanishes if the force passes through the axis or if the force is zero.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.summary: Angular momentum of a particle is defined as l = r × p, where r is position vector and p is linear momentum. Its magnitude is l = rp sin(θ). The time rate of change of angular momentum equals the torque acting on the particle: dl/dt = τ.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.concept_name: Angular Momentum of a System of Particles
concepts.summary: The total angular momentum of a system is the vector sum of angular momenta of all particles: L = Σ(ri × pi). The time rate of change of total angular momentum equals the total external torque: dL/dt = τ_ext. When external torque is zero, angular momentum is conserved.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.concept_name: Conditions for Mechanical Equilibrium of a Rigid Body
concepts.summary: A rigid body is in mechanical equilibrium when both translational and rotational equilibrium conditions are satisfied: (1) ΣF = 0 for translational equilibrium, and (2) Στ = 0 for rotational equilibrium. These six scalar conditions ensure neither linear nor angular acceleration of the body.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.concept_name: Centre of Gravity of a Body
concepts.summary: The centre of gravity is the point where the total gravitational torque on a body is zero. For uniform gravitational fields, it coincides with the centre of mass. For extended bodies in non-uniform fields, these two points may differ. It is the effective point where the entire weight appears to act.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.summary: Moment of inertia I about an axis is defined as I = Σ mi ri², where ri is the perpendicular distance of mass mi from the axis. It is the rotational analogue of mass and measures resistance to rotational acceleration. It depends on mass distribution and axis orientation, not on angular velocity.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.summary: Radius of gyration k is defined by the relation I = Mk², where M is total mass and I is moment of inertia about an axis. It represents the distance from the axis at which the entire mass could be concentrated to produce the same moment of inertia. It is a geometric property of the body and axis.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.concept_name: Kinematics of Rotational Motion About a Fixed Axis
concepts.summary: For rotation with uniform angular acceleration α, the kinematic equations are: ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt², and ω² = ω₀² + 2α(θ − θ₀). These equations are analogous to linear kinematic equations with angular quantities replacing linear ones.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.summary: Newton's second law for rotational motion states τ = Iα, where τ is net torque, I is moment of inertia, and α is angular acceleration. Torque produces angular acceleration just as force produces linear acceleration. This relation holds for rotation about a fixed axis.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.summary: Work done by a torque during rotation is dW = τ dθ, where τ is the torque and dθ is the angular displacement. Total work done equals change in kinetic energy of rotation. The power delivered is P = τω, analogous to P = Fv in linear motion.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.concept_name: Kinetic Energy of Rotation About a Fixed Axis
concepts.summary: The kinetic energy of a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis is K = ½Iω², where I is moment of inertia and ω is angular velocity. This is the rotational analogue of K = ½mv² for linear motion. For combined translation and rotation, total kinetic energy is the sum of translational and rotational parts.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.concept_name: Conservation of Angular Momentum for Fixed Axis Rotation
concepts.summary: When the total external torque on a system is zero, the angular momentum about the fixed axis is conserved: L = Iω = constant. This principle applies to situations like ice skaters spinning, divers in air, and rotating tops. It is the rotational analogue of conservation of linear momentum.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.concept_name: Translational and Rotational Motion of Rigid Bodies
concepts.summary: A rigid body not fixed can have pure translation (all particles same velocity) or combined translation and rotation. A fixed body has only rotation about a fixed axis. Pure translation is characterized by all parts having same velocity; pure rotation by all parts having same angular velocity about the axis.

concepts.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
concepts.summary: Rolling motion combines translation of the centre of mass with rotation about the centre. The condition for rolling without slipping is v_cm = ωR, where v_cm is velocity of centre of mass, ω is angular velocity, and R is radius. Total kinetic energy is K = ½Mv_cm² + ½Iω².

Every two bodies in the universe attract each other with a force directly proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. Mathematically, F = Gm₁m₂/r², where G is the universal gravitational constant.

concepts.concept_name: Gravitational Force between Extended Objects and Point Masses
concepts.summary: A uniform spherical shell or solid sphere with spherically symmetric mass distribution attracts an external point mass as if all the sphere's mass were concentrated at its center. Inside a uniform spherical shell, the gravitational force on a point mass is zero.

The universal gravitational constant G = 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ N m²/kg² was experimentally determined by Henry Cavendish using a torsion balance. The measurement involves balancing gravitational torque against the restoring torque of a twisted wire.

concepts.concept_name: Kepler's First Law - Law of Orbits
concepts.summary: All planets move in elliptical orbits with the Sun at one of the two foci. The ellipse is defined by its semi-major axis (half the longest diameter), and a circle is a special case where both foci coincide at the center.

concepts.concept_name: Kepler's Second Law - Law of Areas
concepts.summary: The line joining a planet to the Sun sweeps out equal areas in equal time intervals. This law arises from conservation of angular momentum since gravity is a central force, and explains why planets move faster when closer to the Sun.

concepts.concept_name: Kepler's Third Law - Law of Periods
concepts.summary: The square of the orbital period of a planet is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis of its elliptical orbit: T² ∝ a³. This relationship applies to all planets orbiting the Sun and satellites orbiting any central body.

concepts.concept_name: Acceleration Due to Gravity at Earth's Surface
concepts.summary: The acceleration due to gravity at Earth's surface is g = GM_E/R_E², where M_E is Earth's mass and R_E is its radius. This can be measured directly and combined with G and R_E to determine Earth's mass (weighing the Earth).

concepts.concept_name: Variation of Gravity with Height Above Earth's Surface
concepts.summary: Acceleration due to gravity decreases with altitude above Earth's surface according to g(h) = g(1 - 2h/R_E) for h << R_E. For larger heights, g(h) = GM_E/(R_E + h)².

concepts.concept_name: Variation of Gravity with Depth Below Earth's Surface
concepts.summary: Below Earth's surface at depth d, gravity decreases linearly as g(d) = g(1 - d/R_E), assuming uniform Earth density. The outer spherical shell above contributes zero force.

The gravitational potential energy of a point mass m at distance r from a mass M is U = -GMm/r (with U = 0 at r = ∞). For a system of particles, total potential energy is the sum of energies for all pairs, following the superposition principle.

The minimum speed required for an object to escape Earth's gravitational pull from the surface is v_escape = √(2GM_E/R_E) ≈ 11.2 km/s. This is independent of the object's mass and depends only on the escape location.

concepts.concept_name: Circular Orbits of Satellites Around Earth
concepts.summary: A satellite in circular orbit at height h above Earth has orbital speed v = √(GM_E/(R_E + h)). The gravitational force provides the centripetal force needed for circular motion. Speed decreases with increasing orbital radius.

concepts.concept_name: Time Period of Satellites - Kepler's Third Law for Earth Satellites
concepts.summary: The orbital period of a satellite around Earth follows T² = (4π²/GM_E)(R_E + h)³, which is Kepler's third law applied to satellites. For satellites very close to Earth's surface, T ≈ 85 minutes.

A satellite in circular orbit has total mechanical energy E = -GMm/(2(R_E + h)), which is negative. The kinetic energy is positive and equals half the magnitude of potential energy. Total energy is conserved throughout the orbit.

concepts.concept_name: Conditions for Bound and Unbound Orbits
concepts.summary: An orbit is bound (closed, elliptical) if total mechanical energy is negative. If total energy is zero or positive, the object escapes to infinity. Satellites are bound systems with negative total energy.

The gravitational force on a mass due to multiple masses is the vector sum of forces from each individual mass, calculated independently. This principle allows calculation of net gravitational force in complex mass distributions.

Gravitational potential at a point is the gravitational potential energy per unit mass. For a mass M at distance r, the potential is V = -GM/r. The potential is a scalar quantity and follows the superposition principle.

Astronauts in orbiting satellites experience weightlessness not because gravity is absent, but because both the satellite and astronaut are in free fall toward Earth with the same acceleration. The normal force on the astronaut is zero.

Elasticity is the property of a body to regain its original shape and size after a deforming force is removed. Elastic deformation is the temporary change in shape or size that disappears when the force is removed. This property is essential for engineering applications like building design and structural analysis.

Stress is the restoring force per unit area developed inside a material when an external force is applied. There are three types: tensile stress (pulling), compressive stress (pushing), and shearing stress (tangential). Mathematically, stress = F/A with SI unit N/m² or Pascal.

Strain is the fractional change in dimension of a body due to applied stress. It is dimensionless. Types include longitudinal strain (ΔL/L), shearing strain (tan θ), and volume strain (ΔV/V). Strain indicates the relative deformation produced in the material.

Hooke's Law states that within the elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain (stress = k × strain). The constant of proportionality k is called the modulus of elasticity. This relationship is linear and holds for most materials under small deformations.

Young's Modulus (Y) is the ratio of tensile or compressive stress to longitudinal strain. It measures a material's resistance to elongation or compression. Y = (F × L)/(A × ΔL). Materials with higher Young's modulus are less deformable and are preferred for structural applications.

Shear Modulus (G), also called modulus of rigidity, is the ratio of shearing stress to shearing strain. G = (F × L)/(A × Δx). It measures a material's resistance to change in shape without change in volume. Shear modulus is applicable only to solids.

Bulk Modulus (B) is the ratio of hydraulic stress (pressure) to volume strain. B = –p/(ΔV/V). It measures resistance to change in volume under uniform pressure from all directions. Bulk modulus applies to solids, liquids, and gases.

Compressibility is the reciprocal of bulk modulus (k = 1/B). It represents the fractional change in volume per unit increase in pressure. Solids are least compressible, liquids moderately compressible, and gases are most compressible (about a million times more than solids).

A stress-strain curve plots stress versus strain for a material under tensile stress. The elastic limit (or yield point) is the maximum stress beyond which permanent deformation occurs. Up to the elastic limit, the material returns to original dimensions when force is removed.

concepts.concept_name: Yield Strength and Ultimate Tensile Strength
concepts.summary: Yield strength (σy) is the stress at the elastic limit where permanent deformation begins. Ultimate tensile strength (σu) is the maximum stress a material can withstand before fracture. The distance between these points determines if a material is brittle or ductile.

Plasticity is the property of a material to undergo permanent deformation without breaking when stressed beyond the elastic limit. Plastic deformation is irreversible; the material does not return to original shape after force removal. Substances like putty and mud exhibit high plasticity.

Brittleness occurs when ultimate tensile strength and fracture point are very close on the stress-strain curve; the material breaks with little plastic deformation. Ductility is the opposite; materials with large gap between these points can undergo significant plastic deformation before fracture.

Elastomers are materials like rubber and elastic tissue that can be stretched to several times their original length and still return to original shape. They do not obey Hooke's law over most of their elastic region and have very large elastic regions without a well-defined plastic region.

Poisson's ratio is the ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain in a stretched or compressed material. It is dimensionless and depends only on the material's nature. For most materials, it ranges from 0.25 to 0.35. It describes how a material contracts or expands in directions perpendicular to the applied stress.

When a material is deformed elastically, work is done against inter-atomic forces and stored as elastic potential energy. The elastic potential energy per unit volume is given by u = (1/2) × stress × strain = (stress²)/(2 × Young's modulus). This energy is released when the deforming force is removed.

When a beam is loaded at the center and supported at its ends, it bends downward. The sag δ = (W × l³)/(4 × b × d³ × Y) depends on the load W, span length l, breadth b, depth d, and Young's modulus Y. To reduce bending, increase depth or use materials with large Young's modulus.

Elastic properties determine structural design decisions such as beam cross-sections (I-shaped beams), rope thickness for lifting, pillar dimensions, and material selection for bridges and buildings. Knowledge of elastic limits and moduli prevents permanent deformation and failure under operational loads.

concepts.concept_name: Pressure as Normal Force per Unit Area
concepts.summary: Pressure is defined as the normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area. It is a scalar quantity with dimensions [ML⁻¹T⁻²] and SI unit pascal (Pa). The fluid always exerts force perpendicular to any surface.

concepts.concept_name: Pascal's Law - Pressure Transmission in Fluids
concepts.summary: Pressure in a fluid at rest is the same at all points at the same height, and any external pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions. This law enables the design of hydraulic machines.

The pressure at depth h below a fluid surface increases according to P = P₀ + ρgh, where ρ is fluid density and g is gravitational acceleration. Gauge pressure is the excess pressure above atmospheric pressure.

Atmospheric pressure at sea level is approximately 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa or 76 cm of mercury. A mercury barometer measures atmospheric pressure by balancing it against the weight of a mercury column of height h, where P = ρgh.
